steps.title: Understand the sentence structure
steps.content:
The sentence reads: "Olympia Brown of ______ an important role in the campaign..." "Olympia Brown of [state]" is the subject; "played" is the verb. Nothing should separate a subject from its verb.
  • Choice A — 'Connecticut played': Subject flows directly into verb — grammatically clean. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Connecticut; played': A semicolon splits the subject from its verb, creating a fragment on each side. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Connecticut: played': A colon introduces lists or explanations, not verbs — incorrectly separates subject from verb. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Connecticut. Played': A period ends the sentence after "Connecticut," leaving "Played an important role..." as a subjectless fragment. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: Only choice A keeps the subject and verb together without any intervening punctuation. That matches choice A — A is correct.
